county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Golden. Golden was first territorial capital (1862-1867). Colorado School of Mines. Mix of mountain and suburban communities. Part of Denver metropolitan area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County Clerk and Recorder in Golden holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Golden was the first territorial capital of Colorado (1862-1867). The Colorado School of Mines in Golden (established 1874) has archives relevant to mining families and engineering history. The Jefferson County Historical Commission and Hiwan Homestead Museum have local collections. Lookout Mountain (Buffalo Bill's grave), Red Rocks, and Morrison document tourism and settlement history. The county includes both mountain mining communities (e.g., Evergreen, Conifer) and suburban plains (Lakewood, Arvada, Wheat Ridge). Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. FamilySearch has indexed some Jefferson County records. The Rocky Mountain News (Denver) and the Colorado Transcript (Golden, from 1866) covered Jefferson County events.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Jefferson County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kiowa County formed 1889 from Bent and Cheyenne counties. County seat is Eads. Named for the Kiowa people. Sand Creek Massacre National Historic Site (1864). Eastern plains ranching and farming. Sparse population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kiowa County Clerk and Recorder in Eads holds records from 1889 (created from Bent and Cheyenne counties -- note that Cheyenne County here refers to the renamed entity, not the original territory county). An eastern plains county with ranching and dry farming heritage. Named for the Kiowa people who ranged through the area. The Sand Creek Massacre site (1864) is in Kiowa County -- this National Historic Site documents one of the most significant events in Colorado Native American history. Check Bent County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records document pioneer settlers. Eads is the only incorporated town. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The Kiowa County Press newspaper. Church records in Eads supplement vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Kiowa County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kit Carson County formed 1889 from Elbert County. County seat is Burlington. Named for scout Kit Carson. Eastern plains farming (Union Pacific Railroad corridor). Homesteading era. German-Russian settlers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kit Carson County Clerk and Recorder in Burlington holds records from 1889 (created from Elbert County). An eastern plains county centered on the Union Pacific Railroad corridor. Burlington was the commercial center and railroad town. The Old Town Museum in Burlington preserves plains heritage. Kit Carson County had significant homesteading activity in the 1890s-1910s, followed by Dust Bowl displacement in the 1930s. German-Russian settlers established farming communities. Check Elbert County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The Burlington Record newspaper is a source for vital records and legal notices. Church records from Burlington and Stratton document rural families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Kit Carson County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: La Plata County formed 1874 from Conejos and Lake counties. County seat is Durango. Named for the La Plata River (Spanish for silver). Denver & Rio Grande Railroad. Fort Lewis College. Southern Ute Indian Reservation. Durango & Silverton Railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: La Plata County Clerk and Recorder in Durango holds records from 1874 (created from Conejos and Lake counties). Durango was founded in 1880 as a Denver & Rio Grande Railroad town and smelter center. The Durango & Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad (originally Denver & Rio Grande) is a historic landmark. The Center of Southwest Studies at Fort Lewis College in Durango is an outstanding regional research center with manuscript collections, photographs, and oral histories covering the Four Corners region. La Plata County has significant Ute history -- the Southern Ute Indian Reservation extends into the county. Animas Museum in Durango has local collections. Check Conejos and Lake counties for pre-1874 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO records document mining claims and homesteads.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for La Plata County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lake County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Leadville. One of the most famous mining towns in US history. Silver boom 1870s-1880s. Hugely diverse immigrant population. Parent to many western slope counties. National Mining Hall of Fame.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake County Clerk and Recorder in Leadville holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Leadville was one of the most famous mining towns in American history -- silver boom of the 1870s-1880s made it Colorado's second largest city. The original Lake County was enormous and many counties were carved from it (Chaffee, Summit, Eagle, Pitkin, Gunnison, etc.). The National Mining Hall of Fame and Museum in Leadville has mining-era collections. Leadville's Herald Democrat newspaper (from 1879) is a rich source. The county attracted hugely diverse immigrant workers: Irish, Cornish, German, Scandinavian, Italian, Slavic, Chinese, and African American (many came from Southern states). Tabor Opera House records, Leadville city directories. Colorado State Archives has county records. The Lake County Civic Center Association preserves local history. BLM GLO mining claims and patents.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Lake County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Larimer County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Fort Collins. Colorado State University (1870). Fort Collins military post heritage. Cache la Poudre River. Agricultural and educational center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Larimer County Clerk and Recorder in Fort Collins holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Fort Collins (established as a military post, 1864) became an agricultural and educational center (Colorado State University, founded 1870 as Colorado Agricultural College). The Fort Collins Museum of Discovery and the Local History Archive at the Poudre River Library have extensive genealogical resources including city directories, newspaper indexes, and photograph collections. The Cache la Poudre River corridor has French fur-trapping heritage. Estes Park and Rocky Mountain National Park area settlement records. The Union Colony at Greeley (nearby Weld County) influenced Larimer County development. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. Colorado State University archives hold agricultural experiment station records and extension agent reports that document rural families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Larimer County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Las Animas County formed 1866 from Huerfano County. County seat is Trinidad. Named for the Purgatoire River (Rio de Las Animas). Hispanic heritage. Coal mining. Ludlow Massacre (1914). Santa Fe Trail. Colorado's largest county by area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Las Animas County Clerk and Recorder in Trinidad holds records from 1866 (created from Huerfano County). Trinidad and the surrounding area has deep Hispanic heritage (part of the Vigil-St. Vrain or Las Animas Grant and Maxwell Grant). Coal mining dominated the economy -- the Ludlow Massacre (April 20, 1914) occurred in Las Animas County during the Colorado Coalfield War between striking miners and the Colorado National Guard/CF&I company guards. CF&I company records (Steelworks Center, Pueblo) document coal miners and their families. Italian, Greek, Slavic, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrant communities are documented in church records, ethnic organizational records, and the Trinidad Chronicle-News newspaper. A.R. Mitchell Museum of Western Art in Trinidad has regional history. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. The Santa Fe Trail crossed through the county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Las Animas County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lincoln County formed 1889 from Bent and Elbert counties. County seat is Hugo. Named for President Lincoln. Eastern plains ranching and farming. Union Pacific Railroad. Dust Bowl conditions in 1930s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County Clerk and Recorder in Hugo holds records from 1889 (created from Bent and Elbert counties). An eastern plains county with ranching and farming heritage. Hugo was a Union Pacific Railroad town and cattle shipping point. The county experienced Dust Bowl conditions in the 1930s. Check Bent and Elbert counties for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records document original settlers. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Lincoln County Museum has local collections. Church records in Hugo and Limon document rural families. The Hugo Range Ledger newspaper is a source for vital records and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Lincoln County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Logan County formed 1887 from Weld County. County seat is Sterling. Named for General John Logan. Union Pacific Railroad town. Sugar beet farming. German-Russian Volga German settlers. Overland Trail heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Logan County Clerk and Recorder in Sterling holds records from 1887 (created from Weld County). Sterling was a Union Pacific Railroad town and sugar beet farming center. Great Western Sugar Company records document immigrant workers (German-Russian Volga Germans were particularly prominent in northeastern Colorado). The Overland Trail Museum in Sterling is an excellent regional history resource. Logan County Genealogical Society has indexed local records. Check Weld County for pre-1887 records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Sterling Public Library has local history collections. The Sterling Advocate newspaper is a source for vital records and community events.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Logan County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Mesa County formed 1883 from Gunnison County. County seat is Grand Junction. Largest Western Slope city. Named for the mesas in the area. Ute homeland until 1881 removal. Fruit orchards (Palisade). Bureau of Reclamation irrigation projects.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mesa County Clerk and Recorder in Grand Junction holds records from 1883 (created from Gunnison County). Grand Junction is the largest city on Colorado's Western Slope and was founded in 1882 at the confluence of the Gunnison and Colorado rivers. The Museum of the West in Grand Junction has regional history collections. Loyd Files Research Library at the Museum of the West is an outstanding genealogical resource for the Western Slope with oral histories, photograph collections, and manuscript materials. Mesa County was Ute homeland until the Ute removal in 1881. Fruit orchards (Palisade peach industry) attracted diverse settlers. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. Check Gunnison County for pre-1883 records. Mesa County Public Library District has local history materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Mesa County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Mesa County Clerk and Recorder, Grand Junction

source_profiles.repository: Mesa County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for Mesa County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.source_name: Mesa County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO Records
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Mesa County including homestead entries and mineral patents; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Mesa County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Mesa County from 1890; 1885 Colorado state census is an important supplement; check parent county for earlier census records

source_profiles.source_name: Ute Nation Records -- Mesa County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA, Denver / Southern Ute Indian Tribe / Ute Mountain Ute Tribe / History Colorado
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-removal Ute records for the Mesa County area including treaty records, agency records, annuity rolls, and correspondence; Ute removal from western Colorado completed by 1881; Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute reservations in southwestern Colorado retain tribal records; NARA Denver holds federal Indian agency records

source_profiles.source_name: Mesa County Historic Newspapers -- Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection
source_profiles.repository: Colorado State Library / Chronicling America / Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized historic newspapers for Mesa County communities; Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection (CHNC) provides free online access to many Colorado papers; obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate notices, and community news
